sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Cultured neurons were treated with ouabain, an inhibitor of the Na+/K+ ATPase. Over 30 minutes, intracellular Na+ increased, intracellular K+ decreased, and the membrane potential became less negative. ATP levels initially fell modestly because the cells attempted to restore ion gradients using other transport processes. Removing extracellular Na+ reduced the rate of Na+ accumulation after ouabain treatment.
questions.stem: What happens to intracellular Na+ after ouabain treatment?

explanations.B: Correct. The plotted intracellular Na+ values rise from 10 to 41 over 30 minutes, consistent with inhibition of the pump that normally extrudes Na+ from the cell.
explanations.A: The graph shows an increase, not a decrease to zero.
explanations.C: The values change substantially over time.
explanations.D: Na+ is not converted into K+ by the pump.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Intracellular Na+, intracellular K+, and membrane-potential magnitude after ouabain treatment.

questions.stem: Why does inhibition of the Na+/K+ ATPase make the membrane potential less negative?

choices.D: Ion gradients that help maintain resting potential begin to dissipate
choices.A: The plasma membrane becomes a lipid-free structure
choices.B: ATP directly becomes a voltage-gated channel

explanations.D: Correct. The pump maintains steep Na+ and K+ gradients that support resting membrane potential. When the pump is inhibited, those gradients weaken, reducing the magnitude of the negative resting potential.
explanations.B: ATP hydrolysis powers the pump, but ATP does not become a channel.
explanations.C: DNA export from the nucleus is unrelated to resting potential.

questions.stem: Why would removing extracellular Na+ slow Na+ accumulation?

choices.A: There is less Na+ available to enter the cell down its electrochemical gradient
choices.B: The Na+/K+ ATPase begins pumping without ATP
choices.C: K+ becomes covalently attached to Na+
choices.D: Ouabain is chemically changed into glucose

explanations.A: Correct. If extracellular Na+ is reduced, the driving force and available source for Na+ entry are reduced. This slows intracellular Na+ accumulation after pump inhibition.
explanations.B: The pump remains inhibited by ouabain and still requires ATP.
explanations.C: Na+ and K+ are ions, not covalently linked products in this process.
explanations.D: The passage does not describe conversion of ouabain into glucose.

questions.stem: What direct role does the Na+/K+ ATPase normally play?

choices.C: It pumps Na+ out and K+ into the cell using ATP
choices.A: It passively diffuses glucose through the membrane
choices.B: It synthesizes mRNA in the cytoplasm
choices.D: It opens the nuclear pore complex

explanations.C: Correct. The Na+/K+ ATPase uses ATP hydrolysis to move Na+ out of cells and K+ into cells against their gradients.
explanations.A: Glucose diffusion is mediated by glucose transporters, not this pump.
explanations.B: mRNA synthesis is transcription and occurs in the nucleus for eukaryotes.
explanations.D: Nuclear pores are unrelated to this membrane pump.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strongly support the idea that ouabain acts directly on the pump?

choices.B: Purified Na+/K+ ATPase shows reduced ATP hydrolysis in the presence of ouabain
choices.A: Ouabain changes the color of the culture medium
choices.C: Cells grow more slowly after 48 hours
choices.D: Neurons express many other ion channels

explanations.B: Correct. Reduced ATP hydrolysis by purified pump protein would isolate the pump as the direct target of ouabain without requiring secondary cellular effects.
explanations.A: Medium color is nonspecific and does not identify the molecular target.
explanations.C: Long-term growth effects could be indirect.
explanations.D: Other channels exist, but their presence does not show direct ouabain action on the pump.

questions.passage: Researchers compared normal red blood cells with cells treated with an aquaporin blocker. Cells were placed in hypotonic medium, isotonic medium, or hypertonic medium. In hypotonic medium, normal cells swelled rapidly, while aquaporin-blocked cells swelled more slowly. In hypertonic medium, both cell types lost volume, but volume loss was slower when aquaporins were blocked. The solute concentration inside the cells was initially similar in all conditions.
questions.stem: Which condition produces the greatest swelling in Figure 2?

choices.D: Both cell types in isotonic medium

explanations.A: Correct. Normal red blood cells in hypotonic medium reach 138% of isotonic volume, the highest value shown. This reflects rapid water influx.
explanations.B: Aquaporin-blocked cells also swell, but less than normal cells.
explanations.C: Hypertonic medium causes shrinkage rather than swelling.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Red blood cell volume after 5 minutes in hypotonic, isotonic, or hypertonic medium with or without aquaporin blockade.

questions.stem: Why do cells swell in hypotonic medium?

choices.D: Water enters because extracellular solute concentration is lower than intracellular solute concentration
choices.B: Water is actively pumped by DNA polymerase
choices.C: ATP is converted into water at the membrane

explanations.D: Correct. In a hypotonic environment, water moves into cells by osmosis because the inside has a higher effective solute concentration than the outside.
explanations.A: Ribosomes do not mediate osmotic solute loss in this scenario.
explanations.B: DNA polymerase is unrelated to water transport.
explanations.C: ATP hydrolysis does not convert ATP into water to drive swelling.

questions.stem: What does slower swelling after aquaporin blockade suggest?

choices.C: Aquaporins facilitate rapid water movement across the membrane
choices.D: Aquaporins prevent all osmosis from occurring

explanations.C: Correct. Blocking aquaporins reduces the rate and extent of water movement in the short time window, showing that they facilitate water flux.
explanations.A: Aquaporins are water channels, not cholesterol-synthesizing enzymes.
explanations.B: They transport water rather than Na+ through voltage-gated pores.
explanations.D: Osmosis can still occur slowly through the bilayer or other pathways.

questions.stem: Which solution would most likely cause crenation of red blood cells?

explanations.B: Correct. Hypertonic medium draws water out of cells, causing them to shrink or crenate. The figure shows reduced volume in hypertonic medium.
explanations.A: Hypotonic medium causes swelling and possible lysis, not crenation.
explanations.D: Pure water is extremely hypotonic and would drive swelling.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the experiment?

choices.D: Aquaporins alter the rate of osmotic water movement but not the direction predicted by tonicity
choices.A: Aquaporins reverse the meaning of hypotonic and hypertonic solutions
choices.B: Aquaporin blockade prevents any water movement at all
choices.C: Cell volume is independent of extracellular tonicity

explanations.D: Correct. The blocked cells still swell in hypotonic medium and shrink in hypertonic medium, but the volume changes are smaller after 5 minutes. Thus aquaporins affect rate, while tonicity determines direction.
explanations.A: The direction of volume change remains consistent with tonicity.
explanations.B: The cells still change volume, so water movement is not fully prevented.
explanations.C: Cell volume clearly varies with tonicity.
